title: Post With Code
text: This is a post with executable code.

title: Building a Pipeline for Crime Data - Introduction
text: This project will take published data from Seattle Police Crime reports. We will be creating a pipeline for this data to be available in a database, and then map out locations for common areas of activity.

title: Building a Pipeline for Crime Data - Introduction
text:
The Tools

Socrata API — API used by government agencies to publish crime data for many different cities across the country
Python and SQL — For the pipeline we will be using Python to transfer into a PostgreSQL
Polars — A DataFrame library used for the transformation

title: Building a Pipeline for Crime Data - Introduction
text:
Why These Tools?
Scorata API is one of the best options for crime logs and statistics, as well as being a significantly more efficient method to obtaining crime data. Polars is used within Python as much more efficient for loading larger data sets than Pandas.

title: Building a Pipeline for Crime Data - Introduction
text:
ETL Process

Run Socrata API to extract the data from the web.
Transform data to be clean and ready for use.
Validate processes.

title: Extract, Load, and Transform - Getting everything I need in one place.
text: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) is pretty standard procedure for data engineering. Breaking it down it is sthe step by step process of getting this data into a database for further use.

title: Extract, Load, and Transform - Getting everything I need in one place.
text:
Extract
Right of the bat, Socrata API becomes one of the greatest assets to extracting Seattle’s crime data. As mentioned before Socrata is used by government agencies to publish crime data. It will scrape Seattle’s public records on the Police department’s website. Because it contains such vast data, I limited the results to just 50,000 results as too much at once can crash less powerful computers. Finished this process by loading it into a Polars dataframe.

title: Extract, Load, and Transform - Getting everything I need in one place.
text:
Transform
Two files are attached to this process (the obvious “transform.py”, as well as validate.py). The first begins by doing a lot of clean up. This amounts to removing duplicates and null/NA type values. It splits our time values into individual unique columns, as well as properly assigning datatypes for latitude and longitude columns. This is vital for better data manipulation which will come in to play with GIS mapping later on. The validate file does exactly what its title would imply; verifies expected columns and their logic.

title: Extract, Load, and Transform - Getting everything I need in one place.
text:
Load
Connecting through the env file, we find the path for the data. Here we get to create the table in a SQL query that will let us have fun later. SQLAlchemy allows to both write the query for the table and the engine to execute.
